blank The living room requires an LED output function \( L(t) \) that supplements natural light \( N(t) \) to reach a constant 500 lumens. Since \( N(t) = 300 + 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) \), the required LED output is \( L(t) = 500 - \left(300 + 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right)\right) = 200 - 200\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{12}t\right) \). This function remains within the 0 to 500 lumen range because the sine term oscillates between -1 and 1, making \( L(t) \) oscillate between 0 and 400 lumens. To find the total energy consumption over 24 hours, we integrate \( L(t) \) from \( t=0 \) to \( t=24 \). The integral of the constant 200 over 24 hours is 4800 lumen-hours, while the integral of the sine term over a full period is zero. Thus, the total lumen-hours is 4800. Multiplying by the power rate of 0.1 watts per lumen-hour yields 480 watt-hours of energy consumption.

blank bedroom lighting combinations must satisfy \( 0.3r + 0.6g + 0.1b = 500 \) with \( r, g, b \) integers between 0 and 255. Solving for \( b \), we get \( b = 5000 - 3r - 6g \). Substituting the maximum possible values for \( r \) and \( g \) (255 each) gives \( b = 5000 - 765 - 1530 = 2705 \), which exceeds the maximum limit of 255. Even with \( r \) and \( g \) at their minimum (0), \( b \) would need to be 5000, which is impossible. Therefore, no valid integer combination of \( r, g, b \) within the 0-255 range can produce exactly 500 lumens using
